questions.stem: A 70-year-old man undergoes elective right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. He has a history of hypertension and takes bisoprolol 5 mg daily. On postoperative day 1, his blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 65 bpm, and urine output is 20 mL over the past 2 hours. He is afebrile, his abdomen is soft and non-tender, and his surgical drains contain minimal serous fluid. His haemoglobin is 110 g/L (pre-op 130). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The absence of tachycardia due to beta-blockade masks the typical response; low urine output, stable Hb, and afebrile state make hypovolaemia from third-space losses most likely. Haemorrhage is unlikely given stable Hb and no drain output; sepsis lacks fever; anaphylaxis has no trigger; MI has no chest pain or ECG changes.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes an emergency laparoscopic cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis. On postoperative day 1, she develops sudden-onset hypotension (75/40 mmHg), tachycardia (120 bpm), and wheeze. She has no rash, no fever, and her abdomen is soft. She has been receiving IV co-amoxiclav since surgery. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acute onset hypotension and wheeze after antibiotic administration is classic for anaphylaxis, even without rash. Haemorrhage would show other signs; septic shock usually has fever/rigors; PE presents with hypoxia/pleuritic pain; MI with chest pain or ECG changes.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension undergoes elective sigmoid colectomy for diverticular disease. On postoperative day 2, he becomes hypotensive (BP 85/50 mmHg) and tachycardic (115 bpm). He has no chest pain or shortness of breath. His ECG shows ST depression in leads V4-V6, and his high-sensitivity troponin is elevated at 85 ng/L (normal <14). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Elevated troponin with ECG changes, no other cause for hypotension, and no bleeding, fever, or allergic triggers point to acute coronary syndrome as the cause of hypotension. Silent presentation is common in diabetics.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on long-term prednisolone 10 mg daily undergoes an elective total hip replacement. On the first postoperative day, she develops hypotension (BP 70/40 mmHg), tachycardia (110 bpm), nausea, and vomiting. She is afebrile, her wound is clean, and her haemoglobin is stable. Serum sodium is 130 mmol/L, potassium 5.0 mmol/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hyponatraemia with hyperkalaemia in a steroid-dependent patient with hypotension and GI symptoms post-operatively is classic for adrenal crisis. Sepsis would likely have fever; haemorrhage would cause Hb drop; anaphylaxis has no trigger; hypovolaemia does not cause electrolyte derangement.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban undergoes an elective inguinal hernia repair. Twelve hours post-operatively, he becomes hypotensive (BP 85/40 mmHg) and tachycardic (110 bpm). His abdomen is mildly distended but non-tender. His haemoglobin has fallen from 130 to 95 g/L. There is no external bleeding. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Significant Hb drop with hypotension and no external bleeding suggests concealed haemorrhage; retroperitoneal bleeding is common in anticoagulated patients. Sepsis unlikely without fever; PE would cause hypoxia; anaphylaxis has no trigger; MI would show ECG changes.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes open right hemicolectomy. On postoperative day 1, he becomes hypotensive (BP 85/50 mmHg), tachycardic (HR 110 bpm), with abdominal distension and tenderness. Which investigation is most likely to confirm intra-abdominal haemorrhage?
options: Full blood count to check haemoglobin drop
questions.explanation: CT abdomen with contrast is the gold standard for detecting active bleeding and haematoma postoperatively. FAST may miss retroperitoneal or slow bleeds; DPL is invasive and less sensitive; Hb drop is delayed; CVP is nonspecific.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman after laparoscopic cholecystectomy develops fever (38.8°C), tachycardia, and hypotension (BP 90/60 mmHg) on day 2. Abdomen mildly distended, WCC 18×10^9/L. Which investigation is most likely to identify the source of sepsis?
questions.explanation: CT abdomen with contrast is best for detecting post-cholecystectomy complications like bile leak or abscess, common sepsis sources. Blood cultures identify organism but not source; others are less specific.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man post laparotomy for perforated DU becomes acutely hypotensive (BP 80/40 mmHg) on day 3, with chest pain, dyspnoea, SpO2 88%. ECG shows S1Q3T3. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: CTPA is gold standard for PE in high-probability cases. D-dimer is non-specific postoperatively; V/Q is less reliable with abnormal chest X-ray; echo shows but does not confirm; Doppler only identifies DVT.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man post partial gastrectomy is hypotensive (BP 90/60 mmHg) and oliguric. Creatinine rose from 80 to 150 µmol/L after 1L crystalloid. Which investigation best differentiates pre-renal from intrinsic AKI?
questions.explanation: FeNa <1% suggests pre-renal, >2% suggests intrinsic ATN. Urine sodium can be affected by diuretics; osmolality is less specific; ultrasound rules out obstruction; BUN:Cr ratio is less reliable.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman on long-term prednisolone for RA post-emergency colectomy remains hypotensive despite fluids, requiring noradrenaline. Baseline cortisol 350 nmol/L. Which investigation confirms adrenal insufficiency?
questions.explanation: Short synacthen test (250 µg tetracosactide IV, cortisol at 30 min) is first-line; peak <550 nmol/L confirms insufficiency. Random cortisol is non-diagnostic; ACTH differentiates cause but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. In the recovery room, her blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 105 bpm. She is warm peripherally, has no evidence of bleeding, and has not passed urine yet. She received 500 mL of IV fluids during surgery. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment for her hypotension?
questions.explanation: The patient has hypovolaemia from inadequate fluid replacement. First-line is IV crystalloid bolus. Colloids are not first-line; vasopressors are not indicated without fluid; furosemide would worsen hypotension; oral fluids are not appropriate in recovery.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is on the ward following an open inguinal hernia repair. He has a patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump delivering morphine. The nurse calls you because his blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg, respiratory rate is 8 breaths per minute, and pupils are pin-point. You have confirmed the diagnosis of opioid overdose. What is the single most appropriate treatment?
questions.explanation: Opioid overdose causes respiratory depression and hypotension. Naloxone is the specific antagonist. Flumazenil is for benzodiazepines; doxapram is a respiratory stimulant but not first-line; fluids alone do not treat the cause; atropine is for bradycardia.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man underwent an anterior resection for rectal cancer three days ago. He develops hypotension (70/40 mmHg), tachycardia (120 bpm), and a rigid abdomen with rebound tenderness. CT shows an anastomotic leak with free fluid. Despite 2 litres of IV crystalloid, his BP remains 80/45 mmHg. What is the most appropriate vasopressor to start?
questions.explanation: He has septic shock. The first-line vasopressor in septic shock is noradrenaline (NICE guidelines). Adrenaline is second-line; dopamine is not recommended; dobutamine for cardiogenic shock; vasopressin is adjunctive.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman had an emergency laparotomy for a perforated peptic ulcer. In the recovery room, the abdominal drain has drained 1000 mL of fresh blood over 30 minutes. Her BP is 80/40 mmHg and HR 120 bpm. A major haemorrhage protocol is activated. Which drug should be administered as first-line to reduce bleeding?
questions.explanation: Tranexamic acid (TXA) is recommended within 3 hours of bleeding to reduce mortality (CRASH-2). She is not on anticoagulants, so vitamin K and protamine are not indicated. Desmopressin is for von Willebrand disease; rFVIIa is reserved for refractory bleeding.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with known severe aortic stenosis undergoes a hemiarthroplasty for a hip fracture. Post-operatively, she becomes hypotensive (85/50 mmHg) and develops chest pain and shortness of breath with a sinus tachycardia of 110 bpm. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in the management of her hypotension?
questions.explanation: Aortic stenosis is a fixed cardiac outflow obstruction; vasodilators such as GTN can cause severe hypotension and collapse. Fluids are cautiously given, vasopressors like metaraminol can help, diamorphine for pain, lying flat may aid preload.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman is 2 hours post-emergency laparotomy for a perforated duodenal ulcer. She is hypotensive (85/50 mmHg), tachycardic (120/min), and has low urine output (<10 ml/hr). The surgical drain has drained 200 ml of fresh blood in the last hour. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Prepare for emergency return to theatre
Request an urgent CT angiogram of the abdomen
Administer a 500 mL crystalloid bolus intravenously
Check full blood count and coagulation screen
questions.explanation: In suspected haemorrhage, the priority is to escalate to the surgical team for definitive haemorrhage control. While fluid resuscitation is important, calling the registrar triggers the necessary team response and preparation for theatre. The most tempting distractor is fluid bolus, but escalation is the more immediate action.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is on day 3 after a right hemicolectomy. He becomes hypotensive (80/50 mmHg), heart rate 110, temperature 38.9°C. His abdomen is distended and tender. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Call the critical care outreach team
Request an urgent CT abdomen with contrast
Check serum lactate and blood cultures
questions.explanation: Given features of sepsis (post-operative, fever, hypotension, abdominal signs), the immediate priority is to start broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour as per NICE sepsis guidelines. The most tempting distractor is lactates and cultures, but treatment should not be delayed for diagnostic tests; antibiotics should be given after cultures, which are part of the bundle but the single most critical action is antibiotic administration.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman undergoes elective inguinal hernia repair under spinal anaesthesia. One hour post-surgery, while lying flat, her blood pressure is 85/55 mmHg, heart rate 48/min, and she is pain-free. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Raise the foot of the bed
questions.explanation: Spinal anaesthesia can cause hypotension with bradycardia due to sympathetic blockade. Atropine is the first-line treatment for bradycardia in this context, as it increases heart rate and improves cardiac output. The most tempting distractor is crystalloid bolus, but atropine specifically addresses the bradycardia which is the primary cause.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is on day 1 post-laparoscopic cholecystectomy, receiving patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) with morphine. He is found drowsy, with pinpoint pupils, respiratory rate 6, blood pressure 85/50, heart rate 55. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: The clinical picture is typical of opioid overdose: respiratory depression, bradycardia, hypotension, pinpoint pupils. Naloxone is the specific antidote and should be given immediately. The most tempting distractor is calling the arrest team, but you can administer naloxone yourself to rapidly reverse the effects while waiting for help.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with a history of COPD taking prednisolone 10 mg daily undergoes an elective sigmoid colectomy. Post-operatively, she did not receive her usual steroid dose. On day 1, she is hypotensive (80/50 mmHg), with low urine output (20 ml/hr), heart rate 65, no fever, no signs of bleeding. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: This scenario suggests adrenal crisis due to chronic steroid use without perioperative stress dose coverage. Hydrocortisone is the immediate life-saving treatment. The most tempting distractor is fluid bolus, but corticosteroids are the priority to counteract acute adrenal insufficiency; fluids are also important but should be given concurrently.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man undergoes open right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. Four hours post-operatively, his blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm. He has a surgical drain in situ. Which single finding most strongly suggests clinically significant ongoing haemorrhage requiring urgent return to theatre?
options: Drain output of 200 mL blood-stained fluid over the last hour
questions.explanation: A drain output of 200 mL per hour of fresh blood indicates active bleeding directly from the surgical site, surpassing other indirect signs (tachycardia, lactate, oliguria, Hb drop) which may lag or be non-specific.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman is day 1 post-emergency laparotomy for perforated diverticulitis. She becomes hypotensive (BP 85/50), with a heart rate of 115 bpm. She has no chest pain and her drain output is minimal. Which single clinical feature should raise suspicion of early sepsis as the cause of her hypotension?
questions.explanation: Acute confusion (altered mental status) is a red flag for sepsis per NICE guidelines, indicating brain hypoperfusion due to systemic inflammation, whereas the other options are common post-operative findings.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man undergoes elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. On day 2, while mobilising to the bathroom, he suddenly becomes short of breath and collapses. His BP is 80/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm. Which single examination finding best differentiates a massive pulmonary embolism from other causes of hypotension?
options: Pleuritic chest pain on deep inspiration
questions.explanation: Raised JVP indicates right ventricular failure from acute pressure overload, distinguishing massive PE from hypovolaemic (low JVP) or cardiogenic (crackles) shock.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is given intravenous co-amoxiclav during a hernia repair. Ten minutes later, she becomes hypotensive (BP 75/40), tachycardic (HR 125), and develops a widespread urticarial rash. Which single finding most urgently signals life-threatening deterioration?
questions.explanation: Inspiratory stridor indicates laryngeal oedema and imminent airway compromise, requiring immediate intramuscular adrenaline, whereas other features are less urgent.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of rheumatoid arthritis, on long-term prednisolone 10 mg daily, undergoes total hip replacement. Twenty-four hours post-operatively, he is hypotensive (BP 85/55) despite adequate intravenous fluids. His temperature is 36.5°C. Which blood test result provides the strongest biochemical clue to the diagnosis of acute adrenal crisis?
questions.explanation: Hyponatraemia is a hallmark of adrenal crisis due to aldosterone deficiency, and is commonly the first laboratory clue, especially in a patient on chronic steroids, whereas hypokalaemia, hyperglycaemia, high cortisol, and alkalosis are not typical.
